title: Movies - The Kathmandu Post
summary: Movies In ‘Ram Naam Satya’, a mysterious vigilante targets the corrupt By Skanda Swar Mycall Chand’s ambitious film mixes spectacle with social commentary, led by a commanding Biraj Bhatta. Four films that defined NIFF’s opening days By Jony Nepal Celebrating world cinema, the 9th Nepal International Film Festival (NIFF) is presenting 88 films and documentaries from 40 countries. content:
Movies In ‘Ram Naam Satya’, a mysterious vigilante targets the corrupt By Skanda Swar Mycall Chand’s ambitious film mixes spectacle with social commentary, led by a commanding Biraj Bhatta. Four films that defined NIFF’s opening days By Jony Nepal Celebrating world cinema, the 9th Nepal International Film Festival (NIFF) is presenting 88 films and documentaries from 40 countries. In ‘Tu Yaa Main’, class is the real antagonist By Skanda Swar What begins as a glossy Gen-Z romance gradually reveals itself as a study of how class shapes intimacy. A battle of rights By Mokshyada Thapa Based on real-life events, ‘Haq’ examines how legal battles intersect with societal judgment and personal resilience. A refugee’s struggle at the heart of ‘Prawasi Jiwan’ By Sanskriti Pokharel The film tries to unpack migration’s psychological toll, even as its storytelling feels rushed in parts. Four Nepali short films to watch on YouTube in 2026 By Jony Nepal From folk horror and coming-of-age stories to questions of caste and spirituality, these films explore identity, belief and relationships through distinct cinematic voices. In ‘Rammita Koo Pirati’, love isn’t sunshine and rainbows By Jony Nepal The film captures the complexities of modern relationships through a woman’s eyes, blending solitude and friendship. In ‘Pariwartan’, action swings between entertaining and absurd By Sanskriti Pokharel Although the film impresses with striking visuals, its overly verbose dialogues and exaggerated violence prevent it from realising its potential. Queer, rural, and all too human stories on screen By Sanskriti Pokharel Showcased at the Queer Film Festival Kathmandu, these short films explore resilience and give voice to stories often ignored in mainstream cinema. Beyond the box office: The 10 Pakistani films that quietly won 2025 By Dawn Beneath the glossy-but-tired commercial surface of Pakistan's film industry, something promising is happening. ‘Eklo-I’ makes science fiction look boring By Sanskriti Pokharel Pradeep Khadka’s screen time does little to impact the story, making his casting feel more like a promotional strategy than a narrative necessity. The cost of staying clean in a dirty system By Sanskriti Pokharel ‘Jhari Pachi Ko Indreni’ tells the story of Ganesh Prasad Sharma, a ward secretary whose honesty in a corrupt system makes him a victim rather than a hero. When control is mistaken for care By Sanskriti Pokharel ‘The Girlfriend’ exposes how romantic relationships can mask control and entitlement as love, showing the gradual erosion of a woman’s autonomy. Struggle against censorship in Nepali cinema By Ganesh Dev Panday Unless our institutions respect artistic freedom, neither Nepali cinema nor our democracy can grow to its full potential. What happens after the violence By Sanskriti Pokharel Showcased at the Nepal Human Rights International Film Festival, ‘Shakti’ follows a single mother and her daughter as they navigate sexual violence, caste prejudice, and institutional indifference in Kathmandu. ‘Homebound’ is Hindi cinema at its bravest By Sanskriti Pokharel It captures the long, punishing journey of migrant workers during the pandemic while exposing the caste, labour and systemic inequalities they live with. ‘Dhurandhar’: Aditya Dhar’s spy saga is as subtle as a troll By Tatsam Mukherjee The hyper-nationalistic drama leaves nothing to chance in its messaging to the audience. ‘Param Sundari’: A tourism ad wrapped as a love story By Sanskriti Pokharel Nowadays, Bollywood movies debut in theatres before moving to streaming platforms. This works well for ‘Param Sundari’ as its appeal lies in scenic charm more than emotional depth. ‘Paran’: Emotional, but often overdone By Sanskriti Pokharel The movie tells a father-children story while weighing the balance between family obligations and personal needs. Cinema that speaks for women By Britta Gfeller What do a rural Italian village, a Nepali hillside home and a Kathmandu living room have in common? At this year’s EU Film Festival, the answer is clear: women fighting to be heard in societies that silence them.

This works well for ‘Param Sundari’ as its appeal lies in scenic charm more than emotional depth. ‘Paran’: Emotional, but often overdone By Sanskriti Pokharel The movie tells a father-children story while weighing the balance between family obligations and personal needs. By Skanda Swar

Mycall Chand’s ambitious film mixes spectacle with social commentary, led by a commanding Biraj Bhatta.

By Jony Nepal

Celebrating world cinema, the 9th Nepal International Film Festival (NIFF) is presenting 88 films and documentaries from 40 countries.

By Skanda Swar

What begins as a glossy Gen-Z romance gradually reveals itself as a study of how class shapes intimacy.

By Mokshyada Thapa

Based on real-life events, ‘Haq’ examines how legal battles intersect with societal judgment and personal resilience.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

The film tries to unpack migration’s psychological toll, even as its storytelling feels rushed in parts.

By Jony Nepal

From folk horror and coming-of-age stories to questions of caste and spirituality, these films explore identity, belief and relationships through distinct cinematic voices.

By Jony Nepal

The film captures the complexities of modern relationships through a woman’s eyes, blending solitude and friendship.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

Although the film impresses with striking visuals, its overly verbose dialogues and exaggerated violence prevent it from realising its potential.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

By Dawn

Beneath the glossy-but-tired commercial surface of Pakistan's film industry, something promising is happening.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

Pradeep Khadka’s screen time does little to impact the story, making his casting feel more like a promotional strategy than a narrative necessity.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

‘Jhari Pachi Ko Indreni’ tells the story of Ganesh Prasad Sharma, a ward secretary whose honesty in a corrupt system makes him a victim rather than a hero.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

‘The Girlfriend’ exposes how romantic relationships can mask control and entitlement as love, showing the gradual erosion of a woman’s autonomy.

By Ganesh Dev Panday

Unless our institutions respect artistic freedom, neither Nepali cinema nor our democracy can grow to its full potential.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

Showcased at the Nepal Human Rights International Film Festival, ‘Shakti’ follows a single mother and her daughter as they navigate sexual violence, caste prejudice, and institutional indifference in Kathmandu.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

It captures the long, punishing journey of migrant workers during the pandemic while exposing the caste, labour and systemic inequalities they live with.

By Tatsam Mukherjee

The hyper-nationalistic drama leaves nothing to chance in its messaging to the audience.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

Nowadays, Bollywood movies debut in theatres before moving to streaming platforms. This works well for ‘Param Sundari’ as its appeal lies in scenic charm more than emotional depth.

By Sanskriti Pokharel

The movie tells a father-children story while weighing the balance between family obligations and personal needs.

By Britta Gfeller

What do a rural Italian village, a Nepali hillside home and a Kathmandu living room have in common? At this year’s EU Film Festival, the answer is clear: women fighting to be heard in societies that silence them.
